title: Lesson 10: Giving and Generosity
description: Breaking the grip of money through tithing and stewardship as acts of worship and trust. This lesson explores why generosity flows from gratitude, not obligation, and why giving is the clearest thermometer of where your heart actually is.

blocks.text: You have spent nine lessons building a foundation. You have encountered the love of the Father, the rescue of the Son, the empowerment of the Spirit. You have explored spiritual disciplines, worship, sin, grace, faith, repentance, and suffering. And now, in the final lesson of this course, you are going to look at something that touches the most personal part of your daily life: your money. Because how you handle your money tells you more about what you actually believe than almost anything else.
Why Jesus Talked About Money So Much

Jesus talked about money and possessions more than almost any other subject - more than heaven, more than hell, more than prayer. Sixteen of His thirty-eight parables deal with money or possessions. He seems to have understood something that most of us resist: money is one of the most spiritual things we handle. It has the power to reveal what we really trust, what we really love, and what we think will ultimately keep us safe.

He was not anti-wealth. Some of His most devoted followers were wealthy. But He was relentless about the heart posture that money tends to produce - the subtle shift from holding things open-handed to holding them so tightly that we forget they were never really ours to begin with. He diagnosed the problem this way:
No one can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money.

Not: you should not. Not: it is hard to. Jesus said you cannot. At some point, every person faces a moment where God's wisdom and money's wisdom point in opposite directions. And in that moment, you discover who is actually on the throne. Generosity is what happens when God wins that moment - when you say, out loud with your bank account, that He is a better treasure than what you are being asked to release.
10.1 - Be honest: is money a source of security for you? What would it take before you would feel unsafe financially? What does that number or condition reveal about where you are placing your trust?
Everything Belongs to God
The foundation of biblical generosity is not primarily about percentages or budgets. It is about ownership. Who does your money actually belong to? Scripture has a clear answer: God. All of it.
The earth is the Lord's, and everything in it, the world, and all who live in it.

This is not a metaphor. God owns the earth. He owns your house, your car, your retirement account, and your salary. You are a manager, not an owner. Everything you have has been entrusted to you - not to keep and protect as your permanent possession but to manage wisely for His purposes during the time you have it. This is what stewardship means.

When this truth settles into you, giving changes completely. It is no longer a sacrifice you make from your pile. It is returning a portion of what has always been His, in trust and gratitude. The question is not, How much of my money should I give to God? The question is, How much of God's money should I keep for myself? That is stewardship thinking. And it is radically freeing.
10.2 - If you genuinely believed that everything you have was entrusted to you by God - that you are a manager, not an owner - what would change about how you make financial decisions?
The Widow Who Gave Everything

Jesus was sitting in the temple treasury, watching people drop in their offerings. The wealthy gave large amounts - visible, impressive, the kind of giving that earns a second look. Then a poor widow came and put in two small copper coins, worth a fraction of a penny.

Jesus stopped everything and called His disciples over. "Truly, I say to you, this poor widow has put in more than all those who are contributing to the offering box. For they all contributed out of their abundance, but she out of her poverty has put in everything she had, all she had to live on" (Mark 12:43-44).

The point is not the amount. It is the proportion - and more importantly, the trust behind the proportion. The wealthy gave from their margin. She gave from her core. She had decided that God was more reliable than the two coins she was holding onto. That is not foolishness. That is faith you can see with your eyes.
Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.

The word cheerful in Greek is hilaros - it is the root of our word hilarious. God loves a hilarious giver. Not a guilty giver. Not a peer-pressured giver. Not a calculated giver who gives the minimum required. A giver who gives so freely and so joyfully that it looks a little crazy from the outside. Because it is. That is the nature of genuine trust.

10.3 - When you give financially - to your church, to missions, to people in need - does it feel more like an obligation or an act of worship? What would change if you approached giving the way the widow approached her two coins?
The Generosity Ladder

Most people start their financial journey as Christians somewhere they are not giving at all, or giving very little. And the path from there to genuine, joy-filled generosity does not usually happen in one jump. Think of it as a ladder with three rungs:

items: Initial Giving - starting somewhere. Any giving. The discipline of taking an amount off the top of every paycheck and giving it before you see what is left. Many people find that starting at 1-2% and growing intentionally over time is the beginning of a transformed relationship with money.

Intentional Giving - giving with a plan. Establishing a percentage goal - the tithe (10%) has been the historic starting point for Christians across centuries. Giving intentionally also means giving to specific things: your local church, missions, the poor, specific needs God puts in front of you.

Irrational Giving - giving that makes no sense from a worldly perspective. This is giving that takes your breath away slightly. Giving that comes from hearing a need and responding without calculating what is left over. The kind of giving that makes people around you look twice - and sometimes look up.

blocks.text: The goal is not to make you feel guilty about where you are on the ladder. The goal is to name where you are and identify where you want to go. Growth in generosity, like growth in every other area of the Christian life, happens one step at a time.
10.4 - Where would you honestly place yourself on the generosity ladder right now - Initial, Intentional, or Irrational? What is one step you could take in the next 30 days to move higher?
Generosity as Worship

Here is the deepest thing this lesson wants you to understand: generosity is not primarily a financial discipline. It is a form of worship. When you give, you are declaring something out loud with your wallet about what you actually believe. You are saying: God is more trustworthy than money. God is a better Father than my bank account. God's Kingdom is worth more than my comfort.

And it is worship that produces something. Paul tells the Corinthians in 2 Corinthians 9:10-11 that God will supply and multiply your seed for sowing and increase the harvest of your righteousness, so that you will be enriched in every way to be generous in every way. This is a generous God blessing generous people with more capacity to be generous. The goal is not your accumulation. The goal is that more and more flows through you to others.

Here is the question that is worth sitting with not just today but as a lifelong habit of stewardship: Are your finances a reflection of your faith or your fear? When you look at what you spend and what you give, what do you see? Faith that God is enough? Or fear that God might not be?
And God is able to make all grace abound to you, so that having all sufficiency in all things at all times, you may abound in every good work.
10.5 - Are your finances right now a reflection of your faith or your fear? As you look at your giving, your spending, and your saving, what does it say about what you are trusting?
This Week's Practice

This week, do one concrete thing with your money that reflects trust rather than fear. It does not have to be large. It might be setting up a recurring gift to your church for the first time. It might be giving a specific amount to someone in need that you have been holding back. It might be committing to a percentage and calculating what that looks like in your next paycheck. Whatever it is - make it a decision of your heart, not a response to pressure.

Also this week, read Luke 12:13-34. Jesus addresses anxiety about money and possessions and then gives the most staggering counterintuitive instruction: "Sell your possessions, and give to the needy. Provide yourselves with moneybags that do not grow old, with a treasure in the heavens that does not fail." Let Him challenge the categories you use for financial security. Let Him tell you what a rich person really looks like.
Completing the Course

You have made it through all ten lessons of Growing Deep in Discipleship. You started with a love for God the Father and you end with a life shaped by His ownership of everything you have. In between, you have walked through identity, the Holy Spirit, spiritual disciplines, worship, the reality of sin, the wonder of justification, the freedom of faith and repentance, the refining fire of suffering, and now the joy of generosity.

These are the roots. They go deep underground where nobody can see, but they hold everything upright. From these roots, the tree grows. The next course - Growing Up in Discipleship - will take you into the branches: the character, the habits, and the relationships that make the tree visible to the world. But first, take a breath. Sit with what God has been doing in you through this series. The roots have gone deeper. That is everything.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
